Metadata.Comment: Scale on successfully-SERVED 2xx-per-task, NOT raw ALB request count. mod_evasive 403-deflects abuse at the Apache layer, so request count is inflated by traffic we reject; scaling on it would add capacity for the abuser. 2xx counts only work actually done -> a real (many distinct IPs) traffic spike scales out; a few-IP abuse storm (deflected to 403s) does not. CPU was historically a no-op here (DB-bound path, Fargate CPU ~11%), but the 2026-06-18 distributed render-scrape of /title+/user pinned CPU at 100% for ~30min while RDS stayed at 0.7ms reads -> FargateAppCpuScalingPolicy now covers that render-bound case alongside this 2xx policy (ECS takes the max desired across both). TargetValue 12000 = ~200 served req/s/task, well under the ~700/s/task load-test ceiling. Deeper ceiling is RDS connections (303), so MaxCapacity caps at 6 (~120 conns); a p99 TargetResponseTime alarm is the real 'DB is the wall' signal.

CPU safety net added after the 2026-06-18 distributed render-scrape, which broke the 2xx policy's 'DB-bound, CPU ~11%, abuse gets 403-deflected' assumptions: a many-IP scrape (spoofed browser UAs, only 2x 403 so mod_evasive's per-IP deflection missed it) of /title+/user pinned Fargate CPU at 100% for ~30min while RDS stayed at 0.7ms reads. As tasks saturated, their 2xx count COLLAPSED into 460s (client-abandoned), so the 2xx-per-target policy read 'load light' and refused to scale out -> both tasks failed health checks and were replaced (the 504 window). This policy scales on app CPU (rises under render load -> correct direction) and runs ALONGSIDE the 2xx policy; ECS uses the max desired across both, so 2xx still governs normal DB-bound traffic while CPU covers render-bound saturation. Target 60% avg CPU; pins -> scale toward MaxCapacity. Post-PSGI, RDS connections are no longer the ceiling (incident peaked at ~43 of 303), so MaxCapacity raised to 8 (~160 conns).

Properties.AlarmDescription: Front-end p99 response time > 1s for 3 min. Real user-facing slowness; on a DB-bound app this usually means the database is the bottleneck -- autoscaling more app tasks will NOT fix it. Investigate RDS / per-page query load.
Target 5xx > 10/min for 3 min. The app is failing requests (caught dies -> 500s, or a regression). Check /aws/events/e2-app-errors and the ALB access logs.
Fewer than 2 healthy app tasks for 3 min -- a task is down or failing health checks (lost N+1 redundancy). Check ECS service events and task stoppedReason.

RDS FreeableMemory < 150 MB for 5 min. Memory is E2's binding DB constraint (the InnoDB buffer pool). Normal floor is ~380 MB; below 150 MB the instance risks swap/OOM -- the working set has outgrown RAM. Lever: bigger instance class or trim the working set. This is the 'memory is the wall' signal Fargate scaling cannot fix.

RDS DatabaseConnections > 250 for 5 min (max_connections=303). Approaching the connection ceiling -- a leak, or too many Starman workers across scaled-out tasks (STARMAN_WORKERS x task count + cron/heavyjob). The connection ceiling, not Fargate, is the real scale-out limit.

RDS CPUUtilization > 80% for 10 min. The DB is CPU-strained, and a burstable t4g.medium burns CPU credits above its ~40% baseline. Likely a heavy/uncached query pattern. Check the slow-query log and the ~130-queries-per-page load.

In-webhead cron has wedged/failing jobs (E2/Cron UnhealthyJobs > 0) for 3 min, OR no leader is publishing the metric at all -- missing data is treated as breaching, so this also catches total cron death (no webhead winning the GET_LOCK and ticking). The runner emits UnhealthyJobs (wedged + failing count from Everything::Cron::Health) each run and during long jobs. Check the [cron] lines in /aws/fargate/fargate-app-awslogs and the cron_state table.

Properties.Description: Cron rule to call generate-sitemap. This heavy ~50min/~1M-node daily batch silently no-ops on the resource-shared in-webhead cron sidecar (it needs a dedicated task's CPU+memory), so it runs as a dedicated Fargate task here. Deliberately kept out of the in-webhead cron registry (Cron/Schedule.pm) so it does not run in both places.
Properties.Name: cron-generate-sitemap
